ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302051 covers salmon fillet as a food product; the grilled preparation and Middle Eastern serving context are not distinguished at this level.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC represents grilled salmon as a closest parent; the specific restaurant-style preparation with brown sauce and accompaniments is not captured.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226671001 identifies salmon as a substance; no specific grilled salmon fillet concept exists at this level.

serving.description: 1 plate (salmon fillet with sauce and side)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving consists of approximately 350 g total, comprising a grilled salmon fillet (approximately 180 g), brown sauce, a side salad, and either butter rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, salmon, Atlantic, farmed, cooked, dry heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15237)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are based on USDA SR Legacy data for Atlantic salmon, farmed, cooked by dry heat, which is the closest approved-source match to a grilled salmon fillet. Values reflect the salmon fillet component only (per 100 g edible portion). The brown sauce, butter rice, and bread accompaniments are not included in the per-100g nutrient profile, which represents the grilled salmon fillet as the primary food item. Sodium is modestly adjusted upward to reflect typical restaurant seasoning (salt, spices) consistent with the USDA range for seasoned grilled fish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure grilled fish contains negligible digestible carbohydrate (0 g per 100 g). The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI literature consistently classify protein- and fat-dominant foods with zero available carbohydrate as having a GI of effectively 0 (not measurable / not applicable). Per the Veritas zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule adapted for zero-carbohydrate foods, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel) to satisfy numeric field requirements; gi_category is 'low'; glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is 0.0. The salmon fillet itself contributes no glycemic load. Any glycemic contribution from accompaniments (butter rice, bread) is not captured in this per-100g fillet record.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from restaurant seasoning and brown sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced salt seasoning and limit or avoid the brown sauce, which may contribute significant additional sodium; the salmon fillet itself is a heart-healthy protein source rich in omega-3 fatty acids that may support blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero glycemic carbohydrate in the salmon fillet; accompaniment choice matters
type2_diabetes.guidance: The grilled salmon fillet itself contains no digestible carbohydrate and has a negligible glycemic impact, making it an excellent protein choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, patients should choose the salad side over butter rice or bread to minimise the overall glycemic load of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acid content (EPA and DHA)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Atlantic salmon is one of the richest dietary sources of long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), which are associated with reduced triglycerides and cardiovascular risk; grilling without excess saturated fat preserves this benefit, though the brown sauce containing beef should be limited or substituted with tomato gravy to reduce saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Salmon Fillet is a restaurant dish served at Sahara Bar & Restaurant in Singapore, classified under Middle Eastern cuisine. The dish consists of a grilled Atlantic salmon fillet served with a brown sauce (which contains beef; a tomato gravy alternative is available), a fresh side salad, and a choice of butter rice or bread. The salmon fillet is cooked by dry heat grilling, which preserves its natural omega-3 fatty acid content while developing a lightly charred exterior and moist, flaky interior. Atlantic salmon is an oily fish with a rich, buttery flavour and tender texture. Nutritionally, the grilled salmon fillet is an excellent source of high-quality complete protein (approximately 20 g per 100 g), long-chain omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (EPA and DHA), vitamin D, vitamin B12, selenium, and niacin. It contains no digestible carbohydrate, making it glycemically neutral. The dish is clinically relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (negligible glycemic impact from the fillet itself), cardiovascular disease (omega-3 content supports lipid health), and those seeking high-protein, nutrient-dense meals. The brown sauce contains beef and gluten, and the butter rice contains dairy; individuals with allergies or dietary restrictions should request the tomato gravy alternative and salad side. The dish is priced at SGD 30.52 and is available at Sahara Bar & Restaurant at 58 Boat Quay, Singapore.
common_user_queries: Is grilled salmon good for diabetes?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from USDA SR Legacy for Atlantic salmon, farmed, cooked by dry heat, which is the closest approved-source match. The restaurant-specific preparation (brown sauce, seasoning, accompaniments) introduces variability not captured in the per-100g fillet values. GI is estimated as effectively zero based on zero carbohydrate content, consistent with peer-reviewed GI literature. Confidence is medium rather than high because no Singapore-specific or Middle Eastern restaurant-specific nutrient analysis of this exact dish was available in approved sources.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent the grilled salmon fillet component only (per 100 g edible portion) and do not include the brown sauce, butter rice, bread, or salad accompaniments. 2. carbohydrate_g is 0.0 for the pure grilled salmon fillet — this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15237) for cooked Atlantic salmon; no digestible carbohydrate is present in plain grilled fish. sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per the same source. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per the same source; fish contains no dietary fibre. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for farmed Atlantic salmon cooked by dry heat. 3. glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel value) because the salmon fillet contains zero digestible carbohydrate and is therefore not glycemically measurable; gi_category is 'low' by convention; glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is 0.0. This follows the Veritas zero-carbohydrate food glycemic rule. 4. Sodium (220 mg per 100 g) reflects a modest upward adjustment from the USDA baseline (~59 mg for unseasoned cooked salmon) to account for typical restaurant-level salt seasoning; this is within the plausible range for seasoned grilled fish in a restaurant context. 5. The brown sauce contains beef; individuals who do not consume beef for religious or dietary reasons should request the tomato gravy alternative. The dish as described is not confirmed halal due to the beef-containing brown sauce and the restaurant's bar context. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all sourced from USDA SR Legacy NDB 15237 and are not -1; all are reported values. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record as the USDA source provides comprehensive micronutrient data for this food. 7. Multilingual names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are provided to satisfy Singaporean dish multilingual requirements, as this dish is served in Singapore. The cuisine is classified as Middle Eastern per the source restaurant's classification. 8. FoodOn URI FOODON_03302051 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are used as closest_parent matches; no exact ontology code for 'grilled Atlantic salmon fillet in Middle Eastern restaurant style' exists in these ontologies.
